utterances: This is the weekly clothes bank, where many of the town's recently-arrived asylum seekers come to kit out their fast-growing children.
Elizabeth Amoah sifts through a pile of woolen sweaters.
The 35-year-old mother of three is from Ghana.
I want my children to be bright future, yeah, because my first child say he want to be a doctor, and the second one say he want to be a lawyer.
Then, my prayer that their dream should come true.
Boss, the name of our Tahoe, is an unbelievable planning machine.
It can do very well in four lanes, on road, off road, dirt roads, traffic, parking and tight maneuvers that many human drivers would have a tough time getting out of.
So it had to do all those things?
Did it have to parallel park?

As it turned out - of course, nobody knew what the competition would be beforehand - but think of how it turned out; the setting was more like a big mall parking lot, where it pooled into parking spots and pooled out.
But if it was presented or confronted with a problem like parallel park, then it does that extremely well.
Mm-hmm.
So you do the 60 mile course, you had a mock city environment and you had to obey very stringent California driving rules, right?
That end, it was interesting to obey speed limits and still have to put together the hottest overall time, which was another amazing matter.
Mm-hmm.
On such - with what those restrictions on short course, pretty phenomenal to gap the field by almost 20 minutes.

Does the car have, in its brain, have to know what the course looks like as it goes through it and figure out what turns it's going to make in advance, or does it go in an intersection and say, well, where do I go now?
All of the above.
Mm-hmm.
So, in the same way that a human driver might start with a general notion of where they were going, maybe something like MapQuest or a familiar route.
The specific circumstances of other traffic, parked cars and pedestrians only reveal themselves when you're in the live action of driving.
So all those things influence driving, whether it's human or robotic.
Mm-hmm.
1-800-989-8255, if you'd like to talk about this great driving machine.
Talking with William ""Red"" Whittaker, professor of the Robotics Institute at Carnegie Mellon.
The whole point of this was to help out the military, right?
Figure out a way to take the soldiers out of the picture, that they don't need to drive these vehicles?
Well, the race organizers are Department of Defense, and there's immense impact for resupply driving trucks, convoys, reconnaissance, ultimately active fire.

But other markets that draw on the technology are agriculture, mining, exploration that - and the big one from this town racing is introducing the technology to the automotive world, which might be the biggest market of all.
Yeah.
We know that the military - a lot of things start out in the military, work their way to consumers.
A lot of things start out in racing and make their way to consumers.
We're talking with William ""Red"" Whittaker, professor in the Robotics Institute at Carnegie Mellon.
Their car, Boss, won the DARPA challenge, and very proud of it.
SCIENCE FRIDAY from NPR News.
So where do you go from here?
Is there - souped-up, or you move on to the next round of challenges, or do you work on just building a better car?
This is what we do.
This is who we are.
And for us, there's always a next step in automation.
One of the most intriguing robotics prizes that's now on the street is the Google Lunar X Prize, which offers 25 million for a robot on the moon.
And we plan to win that one.
You're going to build the rocket too or you just have to build the car?

Actually, anything goes as long - it's very much like the challenge that just occurred, but the general approach in any innovation is that if you can buy it, don't build it or if it's been around, don't reinvent the wheel.
Right.
So there are rockets already.
You don't need to do that.
That's our approach.
You might even find another country that now - the Japanese, the Chinese, you know, the Indians that are in the rocket race.
Well, you should be in business development because the reality is that there is much more of a progressive world that hasn't been there and hasn't done.
And for whom the novelty and the fire and the adventure of exploration would boil the blood.
I always think that if you can get around a parking lot in California and not hit anything, the moon might be a piece of cake.
Well, that I never take anything for granted.
It might be a little presumptuous, but the reality is if we didn't think that it was within the realm of possibility, maybe we wouldn't run.

Something about these great challenges, whether it's Lindberg across the ocean or the first rocket to the moon, or the challenge that just happened this weekend is that before they're accomplished, the general consensus is that it is beyond the technical ability of the day.
And then, the moment they're accomplished, the view is that there was nothing to it.
And a really good challenge is designed that way.
You can't go into it thinking you can't - it cannot be done.
It's almost like stepping into a boxing ring without already having decided that you're going to win.
Well, can you give me an ETA on this?
When we're going to see a touchdown on the moon?
Actually, the Lunar Prize differs from this one.
On the urban challenge, everybody knew that you showed up and went nose to nose on the same day.
And the design of the Lunar Prize is that the first one there to accomplish it wins the prize, so it would be a little too strategic to talk dates.
I think it's important to note Mike first came to the network in the spring of 2015.

Within six months, by October 2015, a colleague of ours, Rebecca Hersher, had formally reported to HR Mike for a dinner which went from supposedly being a moment to counsel her on her career instead to something that very much delved into questions about her personal life in ways that made her feel very uncomfortable.
Then in late September 2015, after what's called a fly in, when a lot of reporters come in from all parts of the country, two veteran editors went to HR to say, hey, we don't have specifics here.
But we know there's a newsroom in which a lot of people are very upset.
In fact, one editor went so far as to say Mike has lost respect of the newsroom and the ability to lead it.
Then in October 2016 was the first of two accusations from a woman who cited Mike's behavior back at The New York Times.
She said he kissed her against her will.
And then again in October 2017 - that is just a couple of weeks ago but a year later - a second woman came in with a separate but almost identical story.
And at that point, NPR started to take action anew, informed the board chairman and the vice chairman and started to look into things.
But there it sat, as far as we know, until The Washington Post published its story.
You've supplemented some of what we know.
Give us a flavor of what you've learned.
Well, so since Oreskes's being put on leave and the next day being forced to resign, I believe an additional five women have come forward to NPR to say - to make complaints of sexual harassment.
I've spoken to nine women.
You know, these are subtler transgressions, perhaps, than that what we've heard from Harvey Weinstein, about Bill O'Reilly, Roger Ailes.
It's not on that scale.
And at the same time, it's a pattern.
It's pernicious behavior.

And time and time again, women told me he made them have to think about themselves in terms of their bodies, in terms of whether they - he somewhat, in ways, was attracted to them - even whether or not they were misjudging the circumstance and taking it too seriously rather than thinking about themselves as professional journalists.
And I think that's one of the real harms done here.
Many questions about how NPR and, certainly, senior leadership has dealt with this situation.
Where does this go from here?
Do we know where the board stands on Jarl Mohn?
They are ultimately the ones who can keep him in his job.
NPR's in the process of commissioning an outside law firm to do a review of what happened in went wrong.
I think that may influence what happens in the time ahead.

People are going to have to decide at the very top level, including the board, whether the mistakes that Jarl Mohn even admits making are significant enough to require new leadership now, or whether he deserves the chance to, in the tail end of his contract, to rebuild trust with the recognition that we've had a number of significant and unexpected leadership changes over the past 10 years.
And those can prove very disruptive and can undercut the progress that's been made journalistically and also in terms of just reaching new audiences, which in Mohn's term, has been pretty impressive.
That's NPR media correspondent David Folkenflik from New York.
David, thanks so much.
Thanks, guys.
The Iditarod sled dog race begins today.
Mushers and their dogs will spend the next week or so trying to cross a thousand miles of the Alaska wilderness.
But as Alaska Public Media's Zachariah Hughes reports, this year, the iconic event is mired in scandal.
(SOUNDBITE OF DOGS BARKING)
Every March, the streets of downtown Anchorage fill with hundreds of sled dogs and mushing fans outfitted in their finest furs.
Then competitors set out on the grueling journey over snowy mountains, icy rivers and frozen tundra toward the tiny town of Nome on the Bering Sea coast.
But this year's race is extra challenging as multiple controversies have crashed down all at once, even driving one of its stars to post a 17-minute video on YouTube.
(SOUNDBITE OF YOUTUBE VIDEO, ""DALLAS SEAVEY STATEMENT"")
The Iditarod can try to run me over.
They can try to throw me under the bus.
But I'm going to be honest to myself.
And they're going to find out I don't fit under the bus.
Dallas Seavey is a mushing wunderkind, having won the race four times before age 30.

But since last fall, he's been embroiled in the sport's first high-profile doping scandal after it came out that some of his dogs tested positive for a banned painkiller at the end of last year's race.
Seavey vigorously denies that he drugged his team and has faulted the Iditarod's board of directors for mishandling the investigation and hurting his reputation in the process.
He pulled out of this year's race, hired a public relations firm and has aggressively defended his record in the press.
(SOUNDBITE OF YOUTUBE VIDEO, ""DALLAS SEAVEY STATEMENT"")
I believe this was given to my dogs maliciously as - that's one of the options.
I think that's the most likely option.
